sentences.text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to give you an example of the life, [INAUDIBLE] my father owned his own place, had people working for him.
As far as I remember, I never seen him work with his hands or with his tools.
When the Russians came, they took that away from him.
They put him to work with his hands.
And one of the men that used to work for my father, [INAUDIBLE], was in charge.
And that's how we--
Yes.
As a matter of fact, we had only Jewish people working in our place.
Why?
I don't know.
There was-- the one time was a neighbor son worked for us.
He wanted to learn the trade.
For about a year, he worked, and then he quit.
Why, I don't remember.
But otherwise there were all Jewish people working there.
Social interactions were about the same as during Polish times.
Of course, times were a little tougher when it came to acquiring food, because very often we had to stay in lines in order to get a bread.
A line, I mean a queue, or whatever you call it.
And the same thing goes for sugar.
To get a two pounds or one kilo of sugar, sometimes had to stand since 5:00 in the morning till late in the afternoon, and-- and they ran out.
And that was the great Soviet system of non-distribution.
In one town they could have sugar.
In the other town, they had bread.
In the third town they had shoes.
And that's how it went.
There was no distribution.
And you could never have a normal way of life.
So what happened at that time?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We-- people that were-- that had things in their house used to trade with the farmers.
Where the peasants used to bring a piece a butter or some flowers or whatever it was, potatoes, milk.
They used to trade a shirt for a container of milk, or a suit for a bag of potatoes.
And that's how things went on during the Russians.
And of course, you could buy almost everything on the black market.
If you. had the money, you could buy a lot of things.
But in the stores, the stores were always empty.
As a matter of fact, when the Russians came in 1939, they-- we had stores, like, you say you go in here to a store.
They were stocked with every.
There was everything you could buy, as long as you had money.
They came in and they bought out.
Within a few days, every store was just bare shelves, and nothing else.
Nothing.
There wasn't one item they wouldn't buy.
And whatever was finished, there was no restocking again, or buying from wherever.
So that was the story.
And whatever you had, the more clothes or jewelry or whatever your had, you could exist.
Jewish holidays were probably observed.
I really don't remember too well.
But I don't think anybody stopped you from observing holidays, or I don't even think anybody stopped anybody going to the temple every day.
I-- I-- I'm not sure about that, but I really can't think of any religious persecution, per se, to that effect.
Of course, in schools they taught you to be a nonbeliever and so on.
But the older people, I don't think they-- they stopped from practicing their religions.
Yes.

Yes, there was no problem, because everybody still-- most everybody still lived in-- in the same neighborhood and the same home, because traditionally, you lived-- it's not that you grow up here and you move away to a different part of the country or the city.
People stuck in the same neighborhoods.
Young people, when they got married, they lived right next door to their parents or grandparents.
And that's why you went out on the street, you had half your family in your neighborhood.
And that remained the same during the Russians.
Some people, of course, they were displaced.
Some were sent to Siberia.
A few-- and they were very lucky.
And some people stayed just the way they were.
Things started to change in Septem-- in June of 1941 when Hitler declared war on Russia.
I think it was June 21st.
We woke up in the morning, and we heard that Hitler crossed the new border with the France and Allies, which were Russia.
And there was great chaos.
And within a couple days, people-- the Russian army, whoever was still-- they-- they didn't stand up to fight at all.
And they were just retreating, and-- by truck, by train, and by any means.
They were just running east.
And at that time, quite a few of our people-- mostly Jews, I would say-- that had any higher positions under the communists [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went with the Russian army.
They went east to Russia.
No, my family never thought of leaving, because our fathers, that generation, they fought in the First World War.
And they were associated with the Austrian army, which was treating the Jews very well.
And most of them said, it's-- nothing can go wrong, because the Germans are very cultured people.
And the worst they're going to do is they're going to make us work.
And there's nothing is going to happen because a very cultural people and there nothing to fear.
How wrong they were, they found out later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as the war progressed, the Germans took hundreds of thousands of Russian soldiers into the prisoner of war.
And within a few days, they occupied our town again.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when the worst things started happening.
They occupied our town, I think, was July 1st.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the next day, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] accumulated from all the neighboring villages.
The Ukrainians came into town with-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] all kind of tools-- hammers, sickles, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] whatever they could get their hands on.
And they were waiting for an OK from the German authorities for a pogrom.
I don't know if you know what a pogrom is.
It's a situation where you kill Jews.
That's the name which remained yet from Tsarist Russia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, of course, the German authorities immediately gave him permission, and then they went wild.
Wherever they caught a Jew, they used to beat him, maim him.
And at that time, about 200 Jews were killed.
Just by the Ukrainians.
The Germans took no part in it.
They were just looking and having fun.
But they took no part.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They pulled Jews out from their homes.
They caught them on the street, wherever they caught them.
This thing lasted about three days.
And they robbed the homes.
They went into the Jewish homes.
They grabbed whatever they could.
And they went back to their villages very happy, and very satisfied.
My family, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we were lucky except my father was caught, and he was beaten up pretty badly.
And-- but he got home somehow.
Why my father was caught-- this is going back already.
I have to go back with a story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- the Russians, the KGB at that time was called NKVD.
And their building, where their-- in their headquarters, in the basement, when the Germans came in, they found about 40 Poles and Ukrainians killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was, again, part of the-- of the permission given to the Ukrainians to take-- to-- to kill the Jews, because they blamed the Jews for everything.
Every Jew was a communist.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time, they grabbed my father.
He should go and make caskets for those people that were killed by the Russians.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then late in the afternoon, somehow he was going home from wherever he was working, and they caught him.
They beat him up.
But they let him go, and he got home.
And we survived at first pogrom.
No, my father was clean shaved, modern dressed.
We had no-- as matter of fact, in the whole my family, some of them were very religious, but none of them were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wore Hasidic clothes or anything like that.
Well, what we felt at that time was fear-- fear on the-- about the unknown, fear of not knowing what's going to happen, how are we going to live.
And-- and after a few days, when they buried the Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people that were killed-- that we still had a cemetery, and they were buried in the Jewish cemetery in town.
And we started-- immediately they formed a Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] committee of a few prominent Jewish people.
And they started asking for-- everybody had to register to go to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the people on the committee had to assign people-- certain people going here, going there to work.
As a matter of fact, I myself, was assigned to building streets, roads.
In 1941, I was 16 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was assigned to build roads.
And we used to take big rocks-- they used to give us big rocks, with a small hammer, and I used to break it up to small pieces.
And that was our work, day in and day out.
Some people were taken to build bridges.
And-- and they were treated terribly.
They were beaten.
The Hasidic Jews, they used to pull them by the beards.
They used to cut their beards off.
They used to drown them and have fun, laugh-- the German soldiers.
There was not even the SS at that time.
There was just the Wehrmacht, the German army.
And they carried on just as badly as the SS.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] At that time, they still let us live in our home.
And-- and November-- that was the first action in July, and I think the second one was in November of 1941.
They asked for all the women, sick people, old people, and children to report to a certain place.
Well, whoever reported and they were taken away on-- on trucks to the forest and shot.
Yes, I knew quite a few people.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then the winter set in, and we had a very severe winter.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The hunger that was going on at the Jewish population, which I never mentioned before.
We had quite a sizable Jewish population that were so poor that before the war-- before the war, they had to get food for Saturday from their neighbors.
They used to live in little huts where the window was maybe a foot away from the floor.
And they had no wooden floors.
There was just dirt on the floor.
And these people that had no possessions, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] during the war, especially of the winter of 1941, '42, suffered the most.
They were dying of hunger.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Typhus broke out.
They were actually dying on the streets.
They were swollen from hunger.
They swelled up.
And many, many didn't survive that first winter under the Germans.
Without them killing them, they just died.
I remember we still had-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had enough food.
Enough.
We survived.
We had food.
We had some-- my father started working for a German that was in charge of the post office, because my father had golden hands.
He could do beautiful things with his hands in the way of little baskets or furniture, or-- and we got a sack of potatoes, for instance, from one guy.
Another guy, which was a real murderer-- he was killing people left and right-- he used to bring us food because my father used to make boxes for him that we used to deliver to somebody else.
He used to make phonographs for him.
So my father made the boxes-- the wooden boxes.
So he used to bring us food once in a while.
So we struggled.
And then we also had some clothes, so you could barter with the farmers or with the-- and things were going on like this till [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] about August, I think, of 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In August of 1942 was the biggest action.
Should I explain what an action is?
An action we used to call where they were gathering the Jews and taking them away, to be shot or whatever, to the concentration camps or whatever other.
At that time, we had no knowledge of any concentration camps, because the Jews couldn't travel any place.
They couldn't have radios.
They had nothing.
When the Germans walked in again in 1941-- I'm going back with my story again-- the Jews had to turn in every fur coat, every piece of fur, or collar, or anything there was.
Every radio had to be turned in to the authorities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Therefore, there wasn't that much to-- to sell or barter anymore.
But still, if you had extra pair of pants, or extra shirt, you got some food for it.
And that's how things went on till '42.
In August of '42, there was the-- SS came in from all over.
And we had an action that they took away about 6000 people at that time-- 6,000 Jews.
It lasted for two days, and then they stopped.
There was a reprieve for a day.
And we thought that was the end already.
But then the following day, it started all over again.
What happened, they used to take the people to the train station.
They had plenty cattle trains.
They used to load them up on cattle trains.
And they took them-- at that time, we knew already-- they took them to Belzec, where they had crematoriums already built.
Well, Christian people who were traveling and going, and maybe somebody had a hidden radio.
You know, news travels a little bit, even though we had-- didn't have too many connections or-- with the Christian people because they were afraid.
And-- and-- but news did travel.
Some people had more connections, and some people and less.
Some people still worked in the oil industry on the oil wells, where-- which was very important for the Germans.
And there they met with their coworkers.

They still also-- which I didn't mention, you see, that we had to wear-- immediately when the Germans came in, in 1941, we had to wear armband, a white band on the left arm with a Jewish star, in blue.
And you were not allowed to go anyplace without it.
At all times you had to wear it.
You didn't wear it, if they caught you, you could have been killed immediately, or sent away someplace.
There was no lighter penalty.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In August of 1942, I saw happen that my father was supposed to have been working in a store in the middle of the town.
The store was completely empty and some German was going to open up a store there, whatever it was.
We had the keys to it.
My father had the key.
Well, we went there.
And we hid in that store, locked up, so nobody-- nobody knew we were there.
Yes.
As matter of fact, one of the aunts was with us, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were sitting there in the attic.
There was a little window looking out on what was going on, and It's really indescribable.
The Germans formed or they opened up an orphanage for little children.
It was a few hundred children.
At that time, they took all those children-- take them away.

They used to bring, from all around-- because we were right on the main street, looking at the main thoroughfare of the town-- they used to bring Jews with long beards, with the tallis on it from the prayer, taking them on the trucks and taking them to the-- pulling and beating and-- and yelling and screaming.
It was like a nightmare that will never go away.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that lasted, like I said, two days.
Then we went back home, and we thought it was all over.
And then when it started again, we ran back to that store, through the fields.
We were very lucky that we got there.
And we survived that biggest action.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they started talking about a ghetto.
Up to that time, we still lived in our homes.
Some of the homes, of the very big homes, were taken away for the German officers.
But most of the people that-- the average home was not that elaborate for them to live on.
So they started talking about ghettos.
And in our town, they formed two ghettos in two different parts of the town.
The ghettos was not enclosed.
There were no walls around it.
Because of the industry-- of the oil industry-- our people still could walk through the town, as long as they had their band on.
And they used to go to work.

Of course, you couldn't walk around during the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But if you went to work in the morning, or you went home back in the afternoon, you were still able, because the industry was spread all over.
There was oil wells all over the place.
